frameworks.name: deep visual encoder feature space
frameworks.one_line: State-of-the-art deep image features stand in for stored memory representations

frameworks.name: continuous-report models of visual working memory
frameworks.one_line: Extends artificial-stimulus recall models to natural-image continuous reports

frameworks.name: set-size models of working memory capacity
frameworks.one_line: Classic artificial-display benchmark the deep-encoder account is tested against

core_question: Can deep-encoder feature spaces explain human visual working memory errors for natural images, and do the same models also account for errors in artificial-display tasks?
key_assumptions: Recall errors are graded along a continuous response space, not categorical
Image-generation algorithms can yield natural response alternatives varying from the stimulus
Stored VWM representations are rich enough to require natural-image stimuli
manipulations: Stimulus class (natural images vs simple artificial displays)
Response alternatives generated to vary continuously from the target
Feature space used to score memory similarity (deep encoders vs baselines)
Set size in the artificial-display domain
key_findings: Deep-encoder features explained coarse-grained and some fine-grained trial-by-trial difficulty in natural-image recall
Reasonable baseline feature spaces failed to capture this difficulty
Deep visual encoders reproduced set-size effects in artificial displays
Deep encoders gave an overall poorer fit to human data in the artificial domain
interpretations: Deep-encoder representations capture meaningful structure of VWM errors for natural stimuli
A single feature-space account does not yet unify natural-image and artificial-display recall
limitations_noted: Deep encoders provide a worse account of human errors in the artificial-display domain
